does: This paper presents PERSA, a reinforcement learning pipeline that adapts large language models to generate programming feedback matching a specific professor's grading style. The method combines supervised fine-tuning on instructor demonstrations with reward modeling from pairwise preferences and Proximal Policy Optimization. PERSA employs parameter-efficient fine-tuning, updating only top transformer blocks and their feed-forward projections, to constrain learning to style-bearing components while preserving diagnostic correctness. The approach is evaluated on three code-feedback benchmarks, demonstrating the ability to align LLM output with target instructor voice while maintaining feedback quality.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in adapting LLMs through human feedback, extending RLHF methods originally developed for general instruction-following to the specialized domain of educational feedback. It responds to challenges in personalization identified in prior work on automated grading and feedback systems, which struggle to maintain both content accuracy and stylistic consistency. The paper leverages insights from mechanistic interpretability of transformers about how different layers encode distinct information types, building on research showing that style and semantic content can be separated across model depth. The parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach draws from recent work on LoRA and adapter-based methods, adapting these techniques to selectively modify only style-relevant parameters.
enables: This work enables future systems for personalized AI-assisted education where automated feedback tools can authentically represent individual instructor voice and pedagogical philosophy at scale. It opens directions for studying what model components encode style versus content across different domains and tasks, informing more targeted fine-tuning strategies. The approach could extend to other feedback-generation contexts requiring stylistic consistency, such as peer review systems or professional editing. It also raises possibilities for multi-instructor adaptation where systems learn to switch between different teaching voices, and for analyzing what students actually respond to in differently-styled feedback, bridging AI adaptation and educational research on effectiveness of different feedback approaches.
